title: Component Data Structure and Referential Integrity
breadcrumbs: Component Data Structure and Referential Integrity
text: Component Data Structure and Referential Integrity Defining the data structure of a component influences how referential integrity constraints are checked. When you compile a component, Uniface may issue a warning: -1074 RelatedEntity missing for integrity control on del/upd of AnEntity . Copy You can ignore this message if the component cannot be used to delete occurrences of AnEntity . Otherwise, you should insert the RelatedEntity in the component structure. Inserting each related many-entity as a child of the one-entity in the component data structure is the easiest way to have Uniface enforce referential integrity constraints. Uniface assumes that an entity has been included in the data structure only to ensure referential integrity if the following conditions are met: An entity has been inserted as an inner entity without its fields. The inner entity's triggers have not been modified There is no reference in the component's Proc to a field of the inner entity. In this case, the read trigger of the inner entity will not fire after a retrieve on the outer entity. Guidelines for Enforcing Referential Integrity Before including a many entity to enforce referential integrity constraints, consider the drawbacks of retrieving many related occurrences. If no deletes are allowed, you do not need to consider referential integrity checks. If the DBMS can support referential integrity checks with database triggers, Uniface recognizes this (via the connector) and leaves it up to the DBMS, regardless of whether you have defined the component entities. However, it is probably safer to include them in the component structure anyway, in case you ever port to another environment. Consider the hitlist problem of running a form from a current form that displays one or more occurrences of the many entity. In this situation, Uniface is forced to build the hitlist first, so it can be more efficient to use the field list. Scroll bars should be disabled for occurrences of the many entity. Related Links Referential Integrity Referential integrity is a standard feature of relational database design that prevents users or applications from entering inconsistent data. Most relational databases have referential integrity rules that you can apply when you create a relationship between tables. Define the Component Structure For services and server pages, use the Define Structure worksheet to add and organize entities and fields in the component structure. Field Lists A component's field list determines how Uniface manages the fields when retrieving occurrences into the component at runtime. The field list includes not only the fields that are part of the component data structure, but also other fields that may be required, for example, because they are referenced in ProcScript or part of a key.
